Linux VMs are uncommon (only 4.8% of vm ostype is Linux): the virtualmachines aggregation shows Windows dominates (95.2%) while image references include 18.04-LTS and ubuntu-pro, so when provisioning Linux tiers plan to use the existing Ubuntu 18.04/Ubuntu Pro images to align with existing images and tooling.

Managed identities are standard on compute (98.8% of VMs use SystemAssigned, UserAssigned): because almost all VMs already have managed identities, provision system- or user-assigned managed identities for the new Linux VMs so Terraform modules and Automation runbooks can access Key Vault and other resources without secrets.

Key Vaults have strong data protection settings (enablesoftdelete True for 100% and enablepurgeprotection True for 100% of vaults): given this tenant convention, store SSH keys, automation credentials, and any secrets in Key Vault with soft-delete and purge-protection enabled when automating VM and patch-management workflows.

Premium OS disks are common (Premium_LRS used by ~63% of disks and P-series tiers appear frequently): because existing disks favor Premium performance tiers, default new Linux OS disks to Premium SSD in Terraform unless cost trade-offs require Standard tiers.

Log Analytics and data collection are widely used (149 workspaces and datacollectionrules/datacollectionendpoints present): therefore route Automation logs, Update Management events, and network telemetry into existing Log Analytics workspaces via Data Collection Rules to match tenant monitoring patterns.

Network security groups and VNets are pervasive (957 NSGs and 205 VNets): since networking is standardized around NSGs and VNets, create subnets with attached NSGs and follow existing peering/naming patterns so new VMs inherit the tenant's segmentation and security controls.

Network flow logging is sparse (only 4 networkwatchers/flowlogs): because tenant-wide subnet traffic capture is not broadly enabled, plan to enable NSG Flow Logs (Network Watcher) or deploy VNet Taps/collector appliances and forward their logs to Log Analytics to meet the compliance requirement to log inter-subnet traffic.

Azure Automation footprint is small and public-accessible (3 automationaccounts, all with publicnetworkaccess True and Basic SKU): therefore provision regionally-appropriate Automation Accounts with managed identities and Update Management enabled, and consider larger SKUs or additional accounts if you need higher concurrency or isolation.

SSH keys are in use (51 sshpublickeys exist) and many container workloads are Linux-only: given the prevalence of SSH public keys and Linux container workloads, use SSH key-pair provisioning in Terraform for Linux VMs and store private credentials securely in Key Vault rather than embedding them in templates.

Frequent resource grouping patterns include compute + networking + logging (multiple maximal itemsets show VMs, NSGs, VNets and Log Analytics together): to fit tenant conventions, design Terraform modules that deploy vnet->subnet->nsg->nic->vm plus log-analytics and managed-identity modules so new tiers match existing architecture and are reusable.
